content.text: Effective alerting starts by watching what users actually experience, not the internal machinery that might explain it.
Symptom-based alerting

pages on-call engineers when a user-visible service objective degrades — high latency, failed agent runs, wrong-tool loops — rather than on every CPU spike or queue-depth wobble that may or may not matter.
What it is
Symptom-based alert design is an alerting philosophy in which alerts fire on
observable degradation of the service's promises to its users

, expressed as service level indicators (SLIs). For an agentic AI service the relevant symptoms are things a user or downstream system can feel: the fraction of agent sessions that complete successfully, end-to-end response latency, the rate of tool-call failures that surface as errors, and answer-quality signals such as guardrail refusals or hallucination flags.
The opposing style,
cause-based alerting

, pages on internal conditions — a saturated GPU pool, a slow vector database, a rising token-usage rate. Causes are essential for diagnosis but poor as page triggers: most cause conditions are transient, self-healing, or simply do not affect users. Symptom-based design keeps causes on dashboards and reserves pages for symptoms that breach an SLO.
Why it matters

Agent services fail in ways classic web services do not. A model provider can degrade quietly, a retrieval index can go stale, a prompt change can send the agent into repeated tool loops that burn latency and tokens without erroring. If you alert on every internal cause, on-call drowns in noise and learns to ignore the pager — the precondition for a missed outage. If you alert only on the handful of symptoms that map to user pain, every page is actionable and trust in the pager is preserved.
Benefits
Every page corresponds to real user impact, so on-call never debates whether an alert is worth waking up for.
The alert set stays small and stable even as the internal architecture changes — symptoms outlive the components that cause them.
SLO-linked symptom alerts tie directly to error budgets, giving a principled basis for page-versus-ticket severity.
New failure modes unique to agents (tool loops, degraded model quality) are caught as latency or success-rate symptoms without a bespoke cause alert.
Postmortems improve because each alert already names the user-facing objective that broke.
Trade-offs and limitations
Symptom alerts tell you that users are hurting but not why — you still need rich cause metrics and traces on dashboards for diagnosis.
Slow-burn quality regressions (subtly worse answers) may not breach a latency or error SLI and can slip past symptom alerts entirely.
Defining a good agent-quality SLI is genuinely hard; naive proxies such as refusal rate can be noisy or gameable.
A single symptom can have many causes, so mean-time-to-diagnose can be longer than with targeted cause alerts.
Symptom alerts depend on accurate SLO targets; a mis-set target either pages constantly or never fires.
How it works

Each symptom is encoded as an SLI: a ratio of good events to valid events over a window. Availability for an agent service is typically the fraction of sessions that complete without a terminal error:

attrs.latex: \text{SLI}_{\text{avail}} = \frac{\text{sessions completed without terminal error}}{\text{total valid sessions}}

content.text: An SLO sets the target, for example
over a rolling 30-day window. The complement of the SLO is the
error budget
. Rather than paging the instant the SLI dips, symptom alerts page on
burn rate
— how fast the incident is consuming the budget:

A burn rate of 1 exhausts the whole month's budget in exactly a month; a burn rate of 14.4 exhausts it in about two days. Multi-window, multi-burn-rate alerts require a fast window (e.g. 5 min) and a slow window (e.g. 1 h) to both exceed a threshold before paging, which suppresses single-spike false positives while still catching genuine fast burns.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Flow from agent SLIs through burn-rate evaluation and routing to on-call

content.text: Instrumentation emits per-session events tagged with outcome, latency, and tool status. A metrics backend aggregates these into SLIs. Recording rules pre-compute the good/valid ratios over each window; alerting rules evaluate multi-window burn rate and emit alerts with a severity label. The router maps severity to page, ticket, or dashboard-only, attaching the SLO and the runbook link.
When to use it
Any user-facing agent service with a defined SLO and an on-call rotation.
Systems where the internal architecture changes often but the user-facing promises are stable.
Teams suffering alert fatigue from a large set of cause-based, low-signal alerts.
Not sufficient as the only mechanism when you also need proactive capacity alerts (disk filling, cert expiry) — those remain cause-based and ticket-routed.
How to implement
Enumerate the service's user-facing promises and pick one SLI per promise (availability, latency, quality).
Set an SLO target and window for each SLI, agreed with product stakeholders, not chosen by engineering alone.
Instrument sessions to emit the good and valid events each SLI needs, with consistent labels.
Write recording rules that compute the SLI over 5-minute, 1-hour, and 6-hour windows.
Write multi-window burn-rate alerting rules with two severity tiers (fast burn pages, slow burn tickets).
Attach a runbook URL and the SLO definition to every alert's annotations.
Route page-tier alerts to the pager and ticket-tier alerts to the work queue; keep all cause metrics on dashboards only.
Examples

# Prometheus recording rules: agent availability SLI
groups:
  - name: agent_slis
    interval: 30s
    rules:
      - record: agent:sessions_good:rate5m
        expr: sum(rate(agent_sessions_total{outcome="completed"}[5m]))
      - record: agent:sessions_valid:rate5m
        expr: sum(rate(agent_sessions_total[5m]))
      - record: agent:availability:ratio5m
        expr: agent:sessions_good:rate5m / agent:sessions_valid:rate5m

# Multi-window burn-rate alert (SLO = 99.5%, budget = 0.005)
groups:
  - name: agent_burn_rate
    rules:
      - alert: AgentAvailabilityFastBurn
        expr: |
          (1 - agent:availability:ratio5m) / 0.005 > 14.4
          and
          (1 - agent:availability:ratio1h) / 0.005 > 14.4
        labels: {severity: page}
        annotations:
          summary: "Agent availability burning error budget fast"
          runbook_url: "https://runbooks.internal/agent-availability"
      - alert: AgentAvailabilitySlowBurn
        expr: |
          (1 - agent:availability:ratio6h) / 0.005 > 3
          and
          (1 - agent:availability:ratio30m) / 0.005 > 3
        labels: {severity: ticket}
        annotations:
          summary: "Agent availability slow burn — investigate this shift"
Real-world example

A customer-support agent platform serving several million sessions a month defines three symptom SLIs: session success rate (SLO 99.5%), p95 end-to-end latency under 8 seconds (SLO 99% of sessions), and guardrail-refusal rate below 2% as a quality proxy. When an upstream model provider degraded, token generation slowed and p95 latency doubled. No component errored — CPU, memory, and queue depth all looked normal, so no cause alert fired. The latency symptom SLI breached its fast-burn threshold within five minutes and paged on-call, who failed over to the secondary provider.

Crucially, the team had previously run more than 40 cause-based alerts and averaged 15 pages a week, most non-actionable. After moving to seven symptom SLIs they dropped to two or three actionable pages a week, and mean-time-to-acknowledge fell because engineers trusted the pager again.
Common mistakes
Paging on raw SLI dips instead of burn rate, so a single 30-second blip pages the whole team.
Choosing a quality SLI that is easy to measure but weakly correlated with user pain (e.g. token count) and calling it a symptom.
Keeping the old cause-based pages alongside the new symptom alerts, defeating the noise-reduction goal.
Setting SLO targets by gut feel rather than from historical performance and product tolerance.
Forgetting that a total instrumentation outage makes the SLI look perfect (no bad events) — you also need a no-data alert.
Best practices
Keep the page-tier alert set under about ten rules; every page must be something a human should act on now.
Use multi-window multi-burn-rate alerts to balance fast detection against false positives.
Always pair a symptom page with a runbook link that lists the likely causes to check.
Add a no-data / absent-metric alert so a broken pipeline cannot masquerade as perfect health.
Review SLO targets and alert precision quarterly against actual incident outcomes.
Performance and scalability

Symptom alerting scales well because the number of rules is bounded by the number of user promises, not by the number of components. Recording rules pre-aggregate SLIs so the alert evaluator scans a handful of low-cardinality series every interval rather than millions of raw session events. Keep SLI label cardinality low — split by a small set of dimensions (region, tier) and avoid per-user or per-session labels, which explode series counts and slow rule evaluation. On a busy service the recording-rule step is the main cost; evaluating burn-rate expressions over pre-computed ratios is negligible.

Security and reliability

The alerting path is itself a reliability dependency: if the metrics pipeline or the alert evaluator is down, symptoms go undetected. Run the alert evaluator with redundancy and add a dead-man's-switch alert — a rule that always fires into a watchdog so silence signals a broken pipeline. Protect SLO definitions and alert rules in version control with review, because a careless threshold edit can silence a real symptom. Ensure the instrumentation cannot leak user content into metric labels; SLIs need counts and outcomes, never prompt text.
Mental model

Think of symptom alerting like a hospital monitoring a patient's vital signs rather than wiring an alarm to every organ. Heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation are symptoms — if any leaves the safe range, a clinician responds regardless of the underlying cause. The cause metrics (blood chemistry, imaging) are what the clinician consults after the alarm to diagnose. You would never page a doctor because a single enzyme ticked up; you page when a vital sign says the patient is in danger.
Choosing and validating a quality SLI

Availability and latency SLIs are mechanical to define, but the agent-specific promise — that the answer is actually good — resists a single clean metric. A practical approach is to build a composite quality SLI from cheap online proxies and periodically calibrate it against an expensive offline judgment. Online proxies include the guardrail-refusal rate, the tool-loop rate (sessions exceeding a step budget), and an explicit user thumbs-down rate. Offline, a sampled set of sessions is graded by an LLM judge or human reviewer, and the correlation between the proxy and the graded score is checked.

A quality SLI is only trustworthy if the proxy tracks the ground truth. Validate it by computing the correlation between the online proxy score and the offline graded score on a held-out sample; if correlation is weak, the proxy is not a symptom worth paging on. Re-validate after every prompt or model change, because a change can silently decouple the proxy from real quality.

# Composite quality SLI with offline calibration
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class SessionSignals:
    refused: bool          # guardrail refusal
    steps: int             # tool-call steps taken
    step_budget: int       # allowed steps
    thumbs_down: bool      # explicit user negative

def online_quality_score(s: SessionSignals) -> float:
    penalties = 0.0
    if s.refused:                 penalties += 0.5
    if s.steps > s.step_budget:   penalties += 0.3   # tool loop
    if s.thumbs_down:             penalties += 0.4
    return max(0.0, 1.0 - penalties)

def quality_sli(sessions) -> float:
    good = sum(1 for s in sessions if online_quality_score(s) >= 0.8)
    return good / max(1, len(sessions))

def validate_against_offline(online_scores, offline_grades) -> float:
    # Pearson correlation; low value => proxy is not a valid symptom
    import statistics as st
    mox, mgr = st.mean(online_scores), st.mean(offline_grades)
    cov = sum((o - mox) * (g - mgr) for o, g in zip(online_scores, offline_grades))
    dx = sum((o - mox) ** 2 for o in online_scores) ** 0.5
    dg = sum((g - mgr) ** 2 for g in offline_grades) ** 0.5
    return cov / (dx * dg) if dx and dg else 0.0

Only page on the quality SLI once its proxy has demonstrated a strong, stable correlation with graded quality. Until then, keep it on a dashboard and alert on availability and latency, which are unambiguous symptoms.
Build the quality SLI from cheap online proxies you already emit, not from a per-session LLM judge in the hot path.
Calibrate the proxy against sampled offline grades and require a strong correlation before paging on it.
Re-validate the proxy after every prompt or model change — a change can decouple it from real quality.
Treat a decoupled proxy as a dashboard signal, not a page trigger, until it is re-validated.
Weight the composite so the failure modes users care about most dominate the score.
Track the offline grade itself as a slow SLO to catch drift the online proxy misses.
Reading a multi-window burn-rate signal

attrs.alt: Multi-window burn-rate decision: page only when fast and slow windows both breach

content.text: The multi-window design pages only when both a short and a long window agree, which is what separates a real, sustained burn from a momentary spike. A one-minute spike lights the fast window but not the slow one, so no page fires; a genuine outage lights both within minutes. This is the mechanism that lets symptom alerts be both sensitive and quiet.

# Convert an SLO + window into burn-rate thresholds
def burn_thresholds(slo: float):
    budget = 1 - slo                     # e.g. 0.005 for 99.5%
    # (multiplier, long_window, short_window, severity)
    return [
        (14.4, "1h",  "5m",  "page"),    # budget gone in ~2 days
        (6.0,  "6h",  "30m", "page"),    # budget gone in ~5 days
        (3.0,  "1d",  "2h",  "ticket"),  # budget gone in ~10 days
        (1.0,  "3d",  "6h",  "ticket"),  # on-track drift
    ]

def is_page(sli_long, sli_short, slo, mult):
    budget = 1 - slo
    return ((1 - sli_long) / budget > mult
            and (1 - sli_short) / budget > mult)

for mult, lw, sw, sev in burn_thresholds(0.995):
    print(f"{sev}: {mult}x burn over {lw} (confirmed by {sw})")
Key takeaways
Alert on user-visible symptoms tied to SLOs; keep cause metrics on dashboards for diagnosis.
Page on multi-window burn rate, not raw SLI dips, to catch fast burns while suppressing blips.
Agent-specific failures (tool loops, provider degradation) surface as latency or success-rate symptoms.
Keep the page-tier alert set small so every page is trusted and actionable.
Add no-data and dead-man's-switch alerts so a broken pipeline cannot hide behind perfect-looking SLIs.
Choose quality SLIs carefully — a weak proxy is worse than no quality alert at all.
